revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Kamiiso torii at Oarai Isosaki Jinja.jpg | caption = Kamiiso torii gate (神磯) of Ōarai Isosaki Shrine. | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]]
[[Sukunabikona]] }} '''Oarai Isosaki Shrine''' (大洗磯前神社, Ōarai Isosaki jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōarai, Ibaraki|Ōarai City]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |date=2017-05-01 |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine - Japan National Tourism Organization |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |url-status=bot: unknown |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170501034955/https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |archive-date=2017-05-01 |access-date=2023-04-07 }} It has three [[torii]] gates leading into the ocean. It worships [[Sukunabikona]] a god of alcohol and medicine.{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Oarai+Isosaki+Shrine&pg=PA73 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}{{Cite web |title=A Study of Shinto: The Religion of the Japanese Nation |url=https://www.routledge.com/A-Study-of-Shinto-The-Religion-of-the-Japanese-Nation/Katu/p/book/9780415845762 |access-date=2023-11-18 |website=Routledge & CRC Press |language=en}}{{Rp|page=29}} and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]].{{Rp|page=29}} Both identified as [[Bhaisajyaguru]] in historical Buddhist traditions of the shrine.{{Rp|page=166}} It was historically a center of "Sea [[Shugendō|Shugendo]]" with many traditions here parallel to the [[Mountain worship]] linked traditional Shugendo..{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}{{rp|102}} The ocean is the Shintai of the shrine, it is a [[kannabi]]. [[File: Oarai isozaki shrine,Oarai isozaki jinjya haiden,oarai-town,japan.jpg|thumb|right|250px| Hall of worship of the Ōarai Isosaki Shrine.]]The shrine was founded in 856 It is also a [[Myojin Taisha]]. It has a nearby related shrine [[Sakatsura Isosaki Shrine]] dedicated to the same deities == Overview == The shrine is believed to have been established in 856 A.D. and was destroyed during the 16th-century war. It was later rebuilt in the 17th century. The shrine has three torii gates, each offering a unique view and environment. The Kamiiso-no-Torii is the most impressive gate, providing a prime location to watch the sunrise as it faces east. As the sun appears on the horizon, its rays shine through the gate, giving it a beautiful red hue. At night, the gate is equally stunning when illuminated by moonlight.{{Cite web |last=Organization |first=Japan National Tourism |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine {{!}} Travel Japan (Japan National Tourism Organization) |url=https://www.japan.travel/en/spot/1459/ |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=Travel Japan |language=en-us}} == Geographic location == Ōarai Isosaki Shrine is located on the [[Kantō region|Kantō]] Plain, northeast of Ibaraki Prefecture, situated next to the [[Pacific Ocean]]. Address: 〒311-1301, 6890 Isohama-chō, Ōarai-machi, Higashiibaraki-gun, Ibaraki-ken, Japan. Plans and satellite views of the sanctuary. {{Coord|36|18|57.07|N|140|35|14.81|E}} Plans and satellite views of the Kamiiso torii gate (神磯). {{Coord|36|18|53.8|N|140|35|22.2|E}} == History == The Ōarai Isosaki shrine has a long history and was established in the year 856. During the war from 1558 to 1570, the shrine was destroyed. However, it was rebuilt in 1690 by patrons, including [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]]. The [[Ibaraki Prefecture]] has designated the shrine as a cultural property. == Characteristics == The Oarai Isosaki-jinja shrine has three torii gates in separate locations. The main torii gate is a huge reinforced concrete structure that is 15.60 m high and 22.42 m wide, located on a path. Behind it, a second Torii rises, and as you pass this second torii, you see the shrine buildings. The third torii, Kamiiso Torii (神磯鳥居), is located on the coast and faces east towards the Pacific Ocean, it faces east to the ocean and the rising sun, representing the spot where the gods landed. The shrine is about two and a half kilometers away from Oarai Station and can be accessed by taxi or bus. . Oarai Isosaki-jinja is dedicated to two deities, Onamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto, who are believed to have created Japan. The shrine also has an impressive worship hall, guarded by statues of frogs instead of the typical lion-dogs. The frog is considered a symbol of good luck in Japan as it sounds like "safe return" or "return of something lost" in Japanese. The ocean is a [[Kannabi]] for the deities == See also == * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Kanpei-taisha]] * [[Beppyo shrine]] == References == {{reflist}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Kamiiso torii at Oarai Isosaki Jinja.jpg | caption = Kamiiso torii gate (神磯) of Ōarai Isosaki Shrine. | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]]
[[Sukunabikona]] }} '''Oarai Isosaki Shrine''' (大洗磯前神社, Ōarai Isosaki jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōarai, Ibaraki|Ōarai City]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |date=2017-05-01 |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine - Japan National Tourism Organization |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |url-status=bot: unknown |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170501034955/https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |archive-date=2017-05-01 |access-date=2023-04-07 }} It has three [[torii]] gates leading into the ocean. It worships [[Sukunabikona]] a god of alcohol and medicine.{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Oarai+Isosaki+Shrine&pg=PA73 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}{{Cite web |title=A Study of Shinto: The Religion of the Japanese Nation |url=https://www.routledge.com/A-Study-of-Shinto-The-Religion-of-the-Japanese-Nation/Katu/p/book/9780415845762 |access-date=2023-11-18 |website=Routledge & CRC Press |language=en}}{{Rp|page=29}} and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]].{{Rp|page=29}} Both identified as [[Bhaisajyaguru]] in historical Buddhist traditions of the shrine.{{Rp|page=166}} It was historically a center of "Sea [[Shugendō|Shugendo]]" with many traditions here parallel to the [[Mountain worship]] linked traditional Shugendo..{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}{{rp|102}} The ocean is the Shintai of the shrine, it is a [[kannabi]]. [[File: Oarai isozaki shrine,Oarai isozaki jinjya haiden,oarai-town,japan.jpg|thumb|right|250px| Hall of worship of the Ōarai Isosaki Shrine.]]The shrine was founded in 856 It is also a [[Myojin Taisha]]. It has a nearby related shrine [[Sakatsura Isosaki Shrine]] dedicated to the same deities == Overview == The shrine is believed to have been established in 856 A.D. and was destroyed during the 16th-century war. It was later rebuilt in the 17th century. The shrine has three torii gates, each offering a unique view and environment. The Kamiiso-no-Torii is the most impressive gate, providing a prime location to watch the sunrise as it faces east. As the sun appears on the horizon, its rays shine through the gate, giving it a beautiful red hue. At night, the gate is equally stunning when illuminated by moonlight.{{Cite web |last=Organization |first=Japan National Tourism |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine {{!}} Travel Japan (Japan National Tourism Organization) |url=https://www.japan.travel/en/spot/1459/ |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=Travel Japan |language=en-us}} == Geographic location == Ōarai Isosaki Shrine is located on the [[Kantō region|Kantō]] Plain, northeast of Ibaraki Prefecture, situated next to the [[Pacific Ocean]]. Address: 〒311-1301, 6890 Isohama-chō, Ōarai-machi, Higashiibaraki-gun, Ibaraki-ken, Japan. Plans and satellite views of the sanctuary. {{Coord|36|18|57.07|N|140|35|14.81|E}} Plans and satellite views of the Kamiiso torii gate (神磯). {{Coord|36|18|53.8|N|140|35|22.2|E}} == History == The Ōarai Isosaki shrine has a long history and was established in the year 856. During the war from 1558 to 1570, the shrine was destroyed. However, it was rebuilt in 1690 by patrons, including [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]]. The [[Ibaraki Prefecture]] has designated the shrine as a cultural property. == Characteristics == The Oarai Isosaki-jinja shrine has three torii gates in separate locations. The main torii gate is a huge reinforced concrete structure that is 15.60 m high and 22.42 m wide, located on a path. Behind it, a second Torii rises, and as you pass this second torii, you see the shrine buildings. The third torii, Kamiiso Torii (神磯鳥居), is located on the coast and faces east towards the Pacific Ocean, it faces east to the ocean and the rising sun, representing the spot where the gods landed. The shrine is about two and a half kilometers away from Oarai Station and can be accessed by taxi or bus. . Oarai Isosaki-jinja is dedicated to two deities, Onamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto, who are believed to have created Japan. The shrine also has an impressive worship hall, guarded by statues of frogs instead of the typical lion-dogs. The frog is considered a symbol of good luck in Japan as it sounds like "safe return" or "return of something lost" in Japanese. The ocean is a [[Kannabi]] for the deities == See also == * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Kanpei-taisha]] * [[Beppyo shrine]] == References == {{reflist}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Kamiiso torii at Oarai Isosaki Jinja.jpg | caption = Kamiiso torii gate (神磯) of Ōarai Isosaki Shrine. | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]]
[[Sukunabikona]] }} '''Oarai Isosaki Shrine''' (大洗磯前神社, Ōarai Isosaki jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōarai, Ibaraki|Ōarai City]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |date=2017-05-01 |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine - Japan National Tourism Organization |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |url-status=bot: unknown |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170501034955/https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |archive-date=2017-05-01 |access-date=2023-04-07 }} It has three [[torii]] gates leading into the ocean. It worships [[Sukunabikona]] a god of alcohol and medicine.{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Oarai+Isosaki+Shrine&pg=PA73 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}{{Cite web |title=A Study of Shinto: The Religion of the Japanese Nation |url=https://www.routledge.com/A-Study-of-Shinto-The-Religion-of-the-Japanese-Nation/Katu/p/book/9780415845762 |access-date=2023-11-18 |website=Routledge & CRC Press |language=en}}{{Rp|page=29}} and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]].{{Rp|page=29}} Both identified as [[Bhaisajyaguru]] in historical Buddhist traditions of the shrine.{{Rp|page=166}} It was historically a center of "Sea [[Shugendō|Shugendo]]" with many traditions here parallel to the [[Mountain worship]] linked traditional Shugendo..{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}{{rp|102}} The ocean is the Shintai of the shrine, it is a [[kannabi]]. [[File: Oarai isozaki shrine,Oarai isozaki jinjya haiden,oarai-town,japan.jpg|thumb|right|250px| Hall of worship of the Ōarai Isosaki Shrine.]]The shrine was founded in 856 It is also a [[Myojin Taisha]]. It has a nearby related shrine [[Sakatsura Isosaki Shrine]] dedicated to the same deities == Overview == The shrine is believed to have been established in 856 A.D. and was destroyed during the 16th-century war. It was later rebuilt in the 17th century. The shrine has three torii gates, each offering a unique view and environment. The Kamiiso-no-Torii is the most impressive gate, providing a prime location to watch the sunrise as it faces east. As the sun appears on the horizon, its rays shine through the gate, giving it a beautiful red hue. At night, the gate is equally stunning when illuminated by moonlight.{{Cite web |last=Organization |first=Japan National Tourism |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine {{!}} Travel Japan (Japan National Tourism Organization) |url=https://www.japan.travel/en/spot/1459/ |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=Travel Japan |language=en-us}} == Geographic location == Ōarai Isosaki Shrine is located on the [[Kantō region|Kantō]] Plain, northeast of Ibaraki Prefecture, situated next to the [[Pacific Ocean]]. Address: 〒311-1301, 6890 Isohama-chō, Ōarai-machi, Higashiibaraki-gun, Ibaraki-ken, Japan. Plans and satellite views of the sanctuary. {{Coord|36|18|57.07|N|140|35|14.81|E}} Plans and satellite views of the Kamiiso torii gate (神磯). {{Coord|36|18|53.8|N|140|35|22.2|E}} == History == The Ōarai Isosaki shrine has a long history and was established in the year 856. During the war from 1558 to 1570, the shrine was destroyed. However, it was rebuilt in 1690 by patrons, including [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]]. The [[Ibaraki Prefecture]] has designated the shrine as a cultural property. == Characteristics == The Oarai Isosaki-jinja shrine has three torii gates in separate locations. The main torii gate is a huge reinforced concrete structure that is 15.60 m high and 22.42 m wide, located on a path. Behind it, a second Torii rises, and as you pass this second torii, you see the shrine buildings. The third torii, Kamiiso Torii (神磯鳥居), is located on the coast and faces east towards the Pacific Ocean, it faces east to the ocean and the rising sun, representing the spot where the gods landed. The shrine is about two and a half kilometers away from Oarai Station and can be accessed by taxi or bus. . Oarai Isosaki-jinja is dedicated to two deities, Onamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto, who are believed to have created Japan. The shrine also has an impressive worship hall, guarded by statues of frogs instead of the typical lion-dogs. The frog is considered a symbol of good luck in Japan as it sounds like "safe return" or "return of something lost" in Japanese. The ocean is a [[Kannabi]] for the deities == See also == * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Kanpei-taisha]] * [[Beppyo shrine]] == References == {{reflist}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]][[Category:kk]][[en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Oarai Isosaki {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Kamiiso torii at Oarai Isosaki Jinja.jpg | caption = Kamiiso torii gate (神磯) of Ōarai Isosaki Shrine. | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]]
[[Sukunabikona]] }} '''Oarai Isosaki Shrine''' (大洗磯前神社, Ōarai Isosaki jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōarai, Ibaraki|Ōarai City]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |date=2017-05-01 |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine - Japan National Tourism Organization |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |url-status=bot: unknown |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170501034955/https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |archive-date=2017-05-01 |access-date=2023-04-07 }} It has three [[torii]] gates leading into the ocean. It worships [[Sukunabikona]] a god of alcohol and medicine.{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Oarai+Isosaki+Shrine&pg=PA73 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}{{Cite web |title=A Study of Shinto: The Religion of the Japanese Nation |url=https://www.routledge.com/A-Study-of-Shinto-The-Religion-of-the-Japanese-Nation/Katu/p/book/9780415845762 |access-date=2023-11-18 |website=Routledge & CRC Press |language=en}}{{Rp|page=29}} and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]].{{Rp|page=29}} Both identified as [[Bhaisajyaguru]] in historical Buddhist traditions of the shrine.{{Rp|page=166}} It was historically a center of "Sea [[Shugendō|Shugendo]]" with many traditions here parallel to the [[Mountain worship]] linked traditional Shugendo..{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}{{rp|102}} The ocean is the Shintai of the shrine, it is a [[kannabi]]. [[File: Oarai isozaki shrine,Oarai isozaki jinjya haiden,oarai-town,japan.jpg|thumb|right|250px| Hall of worship of the Ōarai Isosaki Shrine.]]The shrine was founded in 856 It is also a [[Myojin Taisha]]. It has a nearby related shrine [[Sakatsura Isosaki Shrine]] dedicated to the same deities == Overview == The shrine is believed to have been established in 856 A.D. and was destroyed during the 16th-century war. It was later rebuilt in the 17th century. The shrine has three torii gates, each offering a unique view and environment. The Kamiiso-no-Torii is the most impressive gate, providing a prime location to watch the sunrise as it faces east. As the sun appears on the horizon, its rays shine through the gate, giving it a beautiful red hue. At night, the gate is equally stunning when illuminated by moonlight.{{Cite web |last=Organization |first=Japan National Tourism |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine {{!}} Travel Japan (Japan National Tourism Organization) |url=https://www.japan.travel/en/spot/1459/ |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=Travel Japan |language=en-us}} == Geographic location == Ōarai Isosaki Shrine is located on the [[Kantō region|Kantō]] Plain, northeast of Ibaraki Prefecture, situated next to the [[Pacific Ocean]]. Address: 〒311-1301, 6890 Isohama-chō, Ōarai-machi, Higashiibaraki-gun, Ibaraki-ken, Japan. Plans and satellite views of the sanctuary. {{Coord|36|18|57.07|N|140|35|14.81|E}} Plans and satellite views of the Kamiiso torii gate (神磯). {{Coord|36|18|53.8|N|140|35|22.2|E}} == History == The Ōarai Isosaki shrine has a long history and was established in the year 856. During the war from 1558 to 1570, the shrine was destroyed. However, it was rebuilt in 1690 by patrons, including [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]]. The [[Ibaraki Prefecture]] has designated the shrine as a cultural property. == Characteristics == The Oarai Isosaki-jinja shrine has three torii gates in separate locations. The main torii gate is a huge reinforced concrete structure that is 15.60 m high and 22.42 m wide, located on a path. Behind it, a second Torii rises, and as you pass this second torii, you see the shrine buildings. The third torii, Kamiiso Torii (神磯鳥居), is located on the coast and faces east towards the Pacific Ocean, it faces east to the ocean and the rising sun, representing the spot where the gods landed. The shrine is about two and a half kilometers away from Oarai Station and can be accessed by taxi or bus. . Oarai Isosaki-jinja is dedicated to two deities, Onamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto, who are believed to have created Japan. The shrine also has an impressive worship hall, guarded by statues of frogs instead of the typical lion-dogs. The frog is considered a symbol of good luck in Japan as it sounds like "safe return" or "return of something lost" in Japanese. The ocean is a [[Kannabi]] for the deities == See also == * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Kanpei-taisha]] * [[Beppyo shrine]] == References == {{reflist}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]][[en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]][[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Kamiiso torii at Oarai Isosaki Jinja.jpg | caption = Kamiiso torii gate (神磯) of Ōarai Isosaki Shrine. | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]]
[[Sukunabikona]] }} '''Oarai Isosaki Shrine''' (大洗磯前神社, Ōarai Isosaki jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōarai, Ibaraki|Ōarai City]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |date=2017-05-01 |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine - Japan National Tourism Organization |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |url-status=bot: unknown |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170501034955/https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |archive-date=2017-05-01 |access-date=2023-04-07 }} It has three [[torii]] gates leading into the ocean. It worships [[Sukunabikona]] a god of alcohol and medicine.{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Oarai+Isosaki+Shrine&pg=PA73 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}{{Cite web |title=A Study of Shinto: The Religion of the Japanese Nation |url=https://www.routledge.com/A-Study-of-Shinto-The-Religion-of-the-Japanese-Nation/Katu/p/book/9780415845762 |access-date=2023-11-18 |website=Routledge & CRC Press |language=en}}{{Rp|page=29}} and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]].{{Rp|page=29}} Both identified as [[Bhaisajyaguru]] in historical Buddhist traditions of the shrine.{{Rp|page=166}} It was historically a center of "Sea [[Shugendō|Shugendo]]" with many traditions here parallel to the [[Mountain worship]] linked traditional Shugendo..{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}{{rp|102}} The ocean is the Shintai of the shrine, it is a [[kannabi]]. [[File: Oarai isozaki shrine,Oarai isozaki jinjya haiden,oarai-town,japan.jpg|thumb|right|250px| Hall of worship of the Ōarai Isosaki Shrine.]]The shrine was founded in 856 It is also a [[Myojin Taisha]]. It has a nearby related shrine [[Sakatsura Isosaki Shrine]] dedicated to the same deities == Overview == The shrine is believed to have been established in 856 A.D. and was destroyed during the 16th-century war. It was later rebuilt in the 17th century. The shrine has three torii gates, each offering a unique view and environment. The Kamiiso-no-Torii is the most impressive gate, providing a prime location to watch the sunrise as it faces east. As the sun appears on the horizon, its rays shine through the gate, giving it a beautiful red hue. At night, the gate is equally stunning when illuminated by moonlight.{{Cite web |last=Organization |first=Japan National Tourism |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine {{!}} Travel Japan (Japan National Tourism Organization) |url=https://www.japan.travel/en/spot/1459/ |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=Travel Japan |language=en-us}} == Geographic location == Ōarai Isosaki Shrine is located on the [[Kantō region|Kantō]] Plain, northeast of Ibaraki Prefecture, situated next to the [[Pacific Ocean]]. Address: 〒311-1301, 6890 Isohama-chō, Ōarai-machi, Higashiibaraki-gun, Ibaraki-ken, Japan. Plans and satellite views of the sanctuary. {{Coord|36|18|57.07|N|140|35|14.81|E}} Plans and satellite views of the Kamiiso torii gate (神磯). {{Coord|36|18|53.8|N|140|35|22.2|E}} == History == The Ōarai Isosaki shrine has a long history and was established in the year 856. During the war from 1558 to 1570, the shrine was destroyed. However, it was rebuilt in 1690 by patrons, including [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]]. The [[Ibaraki Prefecture]] has designated the shrine as a cultural property. == Characteristics == The Oarai Isosaki-jinja shrine has three torii gates in separate locations. The main torii gate is a huge reinforced concrete structure that is 15.60 m high and 22.42 m wide, located on a path. Behind it, a second Torii rises, and as you pass this second torii, you see the shrine buildings. The third torii, Kamiiso Torii (神磯鳥居), is located on the coast and faces east towards the Pacific Ocean, it faces east to the ocean and the rising sun, representing the spot where the gods landed. The shrine is about two and a half kilometers away from Oarai Station and can be accessed by taxi or bus. . Oarai Isosaki-jinja is dedicated to two deities, Onamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto, who are believed to have created Japan. The shrine also has an impressive worship hall, guarded by statues of frogs instead of the typical lion-dogs. The frog is considered a symbol of good luck in Japan as it sounds like "safe return" or "return of something lost" in Japanese. The ocean is a [[Kannabi]] for the deities == See also == * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Kanpei-taisha]] * [[Beppyo shrine]] == References == {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Tourist attractions in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Ibaraki Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:C111]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[sn:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[ja:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Kamiiso torii at Oarai Isosaki Jinja.jpg | caption = Kamiiso torii gate (神磯) of Ōarai Isosaki Shrine. | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]]
[[Sukunabikona]] }} '''Oarai Isosaki Shrine''' (大洗磯前神社, Ōarai Isosaki jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōarai, Ibaraki|Ōarai City]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |date=2017-05-01 |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine - Japan National Tourism Organization |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |url-status=bot: unknown |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170501034955/https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |archive-date=2017-05-01 |access-date=2023-04-07 }} It has three [[torii]] gates leading into the ocean. It worships [[Sukunabikona]] a god of alcohol and medicine.{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Oarai+Isosaki+Shrine&pg=PA73 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}{{Cite web |title=A Study of Shinto: The Religion of the Japanese Nation |url=https://www.routledge.com/A-Study-of-Shinto-The-Religion-of-the-Japanese-Nation/Katu/p/book/9780415845762 |access-date=2023-11-18 |website=Routledge & CRC Press |language=en}}{{Rp|page=29}} and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]].{{Rp|page=29}} Both identified as [[Bhaisajyaguru]] in historical Buddhist traditions of the shrine.{{Rp|page=166}} It was historically a center of "Sea [[Shugendō|Shugendo]]" with many traditions here parallel to the [[Mountain worship]] linked traditional Shugendo..{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}{{rp|102}} The ocean is the Shintai of the shrine, it is a [[kannabi]]. [[File: Oarai isozaki shrine,Oarai isozaki jinjya haiden,oarai-town,japan.jpg|thumb|right|250px| Hall of worship of the Ōarai Isosaki Shrine.]]The shrine was founded in 856 It is also a [[Myojin Taisha]]. It has a nearby related shrine [[Sakatsura Isosaki Shrine]] dedicated to the same deities == Overview == The shrine is believed to have been established in 856 A.D. and was destroyed during the 16th-century war. It was later rebuilt in the 17th century. The shrine has three torii gates, each offering a unique view and environment. The Kamiiso-no-Torii is the most impressive gate, providing a prime location to watch the sunrise as it faces east. As the sun appears on the horizon, its rays shine through the gate, giving it a beautiful red hue. At night, the gate is equally stunning when illuminated by moonlight.{{Cite web |last=Organization |first=Japan National Tourism |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine {{!}} Travel Japan (Japan National Tourism Organization) |url=https://www.japan.travel/en/spot/1459/ |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=Travel Japan |language=en-us}} == Geographic location == Ōarai Isosaki Shrine is located on the [[Kantō region|Kantō]] Plain, northeast of Ibaraki Prefecture, situated next to the [[Pacific Ocean]]. Address: 〒311-1301, 6890 Isohama-chō, Ōarai-machi, Higashiibaraki-gun, Ibaraki-ken, Japan. Plans and satellite views of the sanctuary. {{Coord|36|18|57.07|N|140|35|14.81|E}} Plans and satellite views of the Kamiiso torii gate (神磯). {{Coord|36|18|53.8|N|140|35|22.2|E}} == History == The Ōarai Isosaki shrine has a long history and was established in the year 856. During the war from 1558 to 1570, the shrine was destroyed. However, it was rebuilt in 1690 by patrons, including [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]]. The [[Ibaraki Prefecture]] has designated the shrine as a cultural property. == Characteristics == The Oarai Isosaki-jinja shrine has three torii gates in separate locations. The main torii gate is a huge reinforced concrete structure that is 15.60 m high and 22.42 m wide, located on a path. Behind it, a second Torii rises, and as you pass this second torii, you see the shrine buildings. The third torii, Kamiiso Torii (神磯鳥居), is located on the coast and faces east towards the Pacific Ocean, it faces east to the ocean and the rising sun, representing the spot where the gods landed. The shrine is about two and a half kilometers away from Oarai Station and can be accessed by taxi or bus. . Oarai Isosaki-jinja is dedicated to two deities, Onamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto, who are believed to have created Japan. The shrine also has an impressive worship hall, guarded by statues of frogs instead of the typical lion-dogs. The frog is considered a symbol of good luck in Japan as it sounds like "safe return" or "return of something lost" in Japanese. The ocean is a [[Kannabi]] for the deities == See also == * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Kanpei-taisha]] * [[Beppyo shrine]] == References == {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Tourist attractions in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Ibaraki Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[sn:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[ja:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]]

[[File: Oarai isozaki shrine,Oarai isozaki jinjya haiden,oarai-town,japan.jpg|thumb|right|250px| Hall of worship of the Ōarai Isosaki Shrine.]]The shrine was founded in 856 It is also a [[Myojin Taisha]]. It has a nearby related shrine [[Sakatsura Isosaki Shrine]] dedicated to the same deities == Overview == The shrine is believed to have been established in 856 A.D. and was destroyed during the 16th-century war. It was later rebuilt in the 17th century. The shrine has three torii gates, each offering a unique view and environment. The Kamiiso-no-Torii is the most impressive gate, providing a prime location to watch the sunrise as it faces east. As the sun appears on the horizon, its rays shine through the gate, giving it a beautiful red hue. At night, the gate is equally stunning when illuminated by moonlight.{{Cite web |last=Organization |first=Japan National Tourism |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine {{!}} Travel Japan (Japan National Tourism Organization) |url=https://www.japan.travel/en/spot/1459/ |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=Travel Japan |language=en-us}} == Geographic location == Ōarai Isosaki Shrine is located on the [[Kantō region|Kantō]] Plain, northeast of Ibaraki Prefecture, situated next to the [[Pacific Ocean]]. Address: 〒311-1301, 6890 Isohama-chō, Ōarai-machi, Higashiibaraki-gun, Ibaraki-ken, Japan. Plans and satellite views of the sanctuary. {{Coord|36|18|57.07|N|140|35|14.81|E}} Plans and satellite views of the Kamiiso torii gate (神磯). {{Coord|36|18|53.8|N|140|35|22.2|E}} == History == The Ōarai Isosaki shrine has a long history and was established in the year 856. During the war from 1558 to 1570, the shrine was destroyed. However, it was rebuilt in 1690 by patrons, including [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]]. The [[Ibaraki Prefecture]] has designated the shrine as a cultural property. == Characteristics == The Oarai Isosaki-jinja shrine has three torii gates in separate locations. The main torii gate is a huge reinforced concrete structure that is 15.60 m high and 22.42 m wide, located on a path. Behind it, a second Torii rises, and as you pass this second torii, you see the shrine buildings. The third torii, Kamiiso Torii (神磯鳥居), is located on the coast and faces east towards the Pacific Ocean, it faces east to the ocean and the rising sun, representing the spot where the gods landed. The shrine is about two and a half kilometers away from Oarai Station and can be accessed by taxi or bus. . Oarai Isosaki-jinja is dedicated to two deities, Onamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto, who are believed to have created Japan. The shrine also has an impressive worship hall, guarded by statues of frogs instead of the typical lion-dogs. The frog is considered a symbol of good luck in Japan as it sounds like "safe return" or "return of something lost" in Japanese. The ocean is a [[Kannabi]] for the deities == See also == * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Kanpei-taisha]] * [[Beppyo shrine]] == References == {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Tourist attractions in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Ibaraki Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[sn:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] }} [[en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[sn:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[ja:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]]

It was later rebuilt in the 17th century. The shrine has three torii gates, each offering a unique view and environment. The Kamiiso-no-Torii is the most impressive gate, providing a prime location to watch the sunrise as it faces east. As the sun appears on the horizon, its rays shine through the gate, giving it a beautiful red hue. At night, the gate is equally stunning when illuminated by moonlight.{{Cite web |last=Organization |first=Japan National Tourism |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine {{!}} Travel Japan (Japan National Tourism Organization) |url=https://www.japan.travel/en/spot/1459/ |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=Travel Japan |language=en-us}} == Geographic location == Ōarai Isosaki Shrine is located on the [[Kantō region|Kantō]] Plain, northeast of Ibaraki Prefecture, situated next to the [[Pacific Ocean]]. Address: 〒311-1301, 6890 Isohama-chō, Ōarai-machi, Higashiibaraki-gun, Ibaraki-ken, Japan. Plans and satellite views of the sanctuary. {{Coord|36|18|57.07|N|140|35|14.81|E}} Plans and satellite views of the Kamiiso torii gate (神磯). {{Coord|36|18|53.8|N|140|35|22.2|E}} == History == The Ōarai Isosaki shrine has a long history and was established in the year 856. During the war from 1558 to 1570, the shrine was destroyed. However, it was rebuilt in 1690 by patrons, including [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]]. The [[Ibaraki Prefecture]] has designated the shrine as a cultural property. == Characteristics == The Oarai Isosaki-jinja shrine has three torii gates in separate locations. The main torii gate is a huge reinforced concrete structure that is 15.60 m high and 22.42 m wide, located on a path. Behind it, a second Torii rises, and as you pass this second torii, you see the shrine buildings. The third torii, Kamiiso Torii (神磯鳥居), is located on the coast and faces east towards the Pacific Ocean, it faces east to the ocean and the rising sun, representing the spot where the gods landed. The shrine is about two and a half kilometers away from Oarai Station and can be accessed by taxi or bus. . Oarai Isosaki-jinja is dedicated to two deities, Onamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto, who are believed to have created Japan. The shrine also has an impressive worship hall, guarded by statues of frogs instead of the typical lion-dogs. The frog is considered a symbol of good luck in Japan as it sounds like "safe return" or "return of something lost" in Japanese. The ocean is a [[Kannabi]] for the deities == See also == * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Kanpei-taisha]] * [[Beppyo shrine]] == References == {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Tourist attractions in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Ibaraki Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[ja:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[Category:Ja import failed]]

At night, the gate is equally stunning when illuminated by moonlight.{{Cite web |last=Organization |first=Japan National Tourism |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine {{!}} Travel Japan (Japan National Tourism Organization) |url=https://www.japan.travel/en/spot/1459/ |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=Travel Japan |language=en-us}} == Geographic location == Ōarai Isosaki Shrine is located on the [[Kantō region|Kantō]] Plain, northeast of Ibaraki Prefecture, situated next to the [[Pacific Ocean]]. The frog is considered a symbol of good luck in Japan as it sounds like "safe return" or "return of something lost" in Japanese. The ocean is a [[Kannabi]] for the deities == See also == * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Kanpei-taisha]] * [[Beppyo shrine]] == References == {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Tourist attractions in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Ibaraki Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[sn:arai Isosaki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Kamiiso torii at Oarai Isosaki Jinja.jpg | caption = Kamiiso torii gate (神磯) of Ōarai Isosaki Shrine. | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]]
[[Sukunabikona]] }} '''Oarai Isosaki Shrine''' (大洗磯前神社, Ōarai Isosaki jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōarai, Ibaraki|Ōarai City]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |date=2017-05-01 |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine - Japan National Tourism Organization |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |url-status=bot: unknown |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170501034955/https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |archive-date=2017-05-01 |access-date=2023-04-07 }} It has three [[torii]] gates leading into the ocean. It worships [[Sukunabikona]] a god of alcohol and medicine.{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Oarai+Isosaki+Shrine&pg=PA73 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}{{Cite web |title=A Study of Shinto: The Religion of the Japanese Nation |url=https://www.routledge.com/A-Study-of-Shinto-The-Religion-of-the-Japanese-Nation/Katu/p/book/9780415845762 |access-date=2023-11-18 |website=Routledge & CRC Press |language=en}}{{Rp|page=29}} and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]].{{Rp|page=29}} Both identified as [[Bhaisajyaguru]] in historical Buddhist traditions of the shrine.{{Rp|page=166}} It was historically a center of "Sea [[Shugendō|Shugendo]]" with many traditions here parallel to the [[Mountain worship]] linked traditional Shugendo..{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}{{rp|102}} The ocean is the Shintai of the shrine, it is a [[kannabi]]. [[File: Oarai isozaki shrine,Oarai isozaki jinjya haiden,oarai-town,japan.jpg|thumb|right|250px| Hall of worship of the Ōarai Isosaki Shrine.]]The shrine was founded in 856 It is also a [[Myojin Taisha]]. It has a nearby related shrine [[Sakatsura Isosaki Shrine]] dedicated to the same deities == Overview == The shrine is believed to have been established in 856 A.D. and was destroyed during the 16th-century war. It was later rebuilt in the 17th century. The shrine has three torii gates, each offering a unique view and environment. The Kamiiso-no-Torii is the most impressive gate, providing a prime location to watch the sunrise as it faces east. As the sun appears on the horizon, its rays shine through the gate, giving it a beautiful red hue. At night, the gate is equally stunning when illuminated by moonlight.{{Cite web |last=Organization |first=Japan National Tourism |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine {{!}} Travel Japan (Japan National Tourism Organization) |url=https://www.japan.travel/en/spot/1459/ |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=Travel Japan |language=en-us}} == Geographic location == Ōarai Isosaki Shrine is located on the [[Kantō region|Kantō]] Plain, northeast of Ibaraki Prefecture, situated next to the [[Pacific Ocean]]. Address: 〒311-1301, 6890 Isohama-chō, Ōarai-machi, Higashiibaraki-gun, Ibaraki-ken, Japan. Plans and satellite views of the sanctuary. {{Coord|36|18|57.07|N|140|35|14.81|E}} Plans and satellite views of the Kamiiso torii gate (神磯). {{Coord|36|18|53.8|N|140|35|22.2|E}} == History == The Ōarai Isosaki shrine has a long history and was established in the year 856. During the war from 1558 to 1570, the shrine was destroyed. However, it was rebuilt in 1690 by patrons, including [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]]. The [[Ibaraki Prefecture]] has designated the shrine as a cultural property. == Characteristics == The Oarai Isosaki-jinja shrine has three torii gates in separate locations. The main torii gate is a huge reinforced concrete structure that is 15.60 m high and 22.42 m wide, located on a path. Behind it, a second Torii rises, and as you pass this second torii, you see the shrine buildings. The third torii, Kamiiso Torii (神磯鳥居), is located on the coast and faces east towards the Pacific Ocean, it faces east to the ocean and the rising sun, representing the spot where the gods landed. The shrine is about two and a half kilometers away from Oarai Station and can be accessed by taxi or bus. . Oarai Isosaki-jinja is dedicated to two deities, Onamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto, who are believed to have created Japan. The shrine also has an impressive worship hall, guarded by statues of frogs instead of the typical lion-dogs. The frog is considered a symbol of good luck in Japan as it sounds like "safe return" or "return of something lost" in Japanese. The ocean is a [[Kannabi]] for the deities == See also == * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Kanpei-taisha]] * [[Beppyo shrine]] == References == {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Tourist attractions in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Ibaraki Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[sn:arai Isosaki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Kamiiso torii at Oarai Isosaki Jinja.jpg | caption = Kamiiso torii gate (神磯) of Ōarai Isosaki Shrine. | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]]
[[Sukunabikona]] }} '''Oarai Isosaki Shrine''' (大洗磯前神社, Ōarai Isosaki jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ōarai, Ibaraki|Ōarai City]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |date=2017-05-01 |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine - Japan National Tourism Organization |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |url-status=bot: unknown |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170501034955/https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |archive-date=2017-05-01 |access-date=2023-04-07 }} It has three [[torii]] gates leading into the ocean. It worships [[Sukunabikona]] a god of alcohol and medicine.{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Oarai+Isosaki+Shrine&pg=PA73 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}{{Cite web |title=A Study of Shinto: The Religion of the Japanese Nation |url=https://www.routledge.com/A-Study-of-Shinto-The-Religion-of-the-Japanese-Nation/Katu/p/book/9780415845762 |access-date=2023-11-18 |website=Routledge & CRC Press |language=en}}{{Rp|page=29}} and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]].{{Rp|page=29}} Both identified as [[Bhaisajyaguru]] in historical Buddhist traditions of the shrine.{{Rp|page=166}} It was historically a center of "Sea [[Shugendō|Shugendo]]" with many traditions here parallel to the [[Mountain worship]] linked traditional Shugendo..{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}{{rp|102}} The ocean is the Shintai of the shrine, it is a [[kannabi]]. [[File: Oarai isozaki shrine,Oarai isozaki jinjya haiden,oarai-town,japan.jpg|thumb|right|250px| Hall of worship of the Ōarai Isosaki Shrine.]]The shrine was founded in 856 It is also a [[Myojin Taisha]]. It has a nearby related shrine [[Sakatsura Isosaki Shrine]] dedicated to the same deities == Overview == The shrine is believed to have been established in 856 A.D. and was destroyed during the 16th-century war. It was later rebuilt in the 17th century. The shrine has three torii gates, each offering a unique view and environment. The Kamiiso-no-Torii is the most impressive gate, providing a prime location to watch the sunrise as it faces east. As the sun appears on the horizon, its rays shine through the gate, giving it a beautiful red hue. At night, the gate is equally stunning when illuminated by moonlight.{{Cite web |last=Organization |first=Japan National Tourism |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine {{!}} Travel Japan (Japan National Tourism Organization) |url=https://www.japan.travel/en/spot/1459/ |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=Travel Japan |language=en-us}} == Geographic location == Ōarai Isosaki Shrine is located on the [[Kantō region|Kantō]] Plain, northeast of Ibaraki Prefecture, situated next to the [[Pacific Ocean]]. Address: 〒311-1301, 6890 Isohama-chō, Ōarai-machi, Higashiibaraki-gun, Ibaraki-ken, Japan. Plans and satellite views of the sanctuary. {{Coord|36|18|57.07|N|140|35|14.81|E}} Plans and satellite views of the Kamiiso torii gate (神磯). {{Coord|36|18|53.8|N|140|35|22.2|E}} == History == The Ōarai Isosaki shrine has a long history and was established in the year 856. During the war from 1558 to 1570, the shrine was destroyed. However, it was rebuilt in 1690 by patrons, including [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]]. The [[Ibaraki Prefecture]] has designated the shrine as a cultural property. == Characteristics == The Oarai Isosaki-jinja shrine has three torii gates in separate locations. The main torii gate is a huge reinforced concrete structure that is 15.60 m high and 22.42 m wide, located on a path. Behind it, a second Torii rises, and as you pass this second torii, you see the shrine buildings. The third torii, Kamiiso Torii (神磯鳥居), is located on the coast and faces east towards the Pacific Ocean, it faces east to the ocean and the rising sun, representing the spot where the gods landed. The shrine is about two and a half kilometers away from Oarai Station and can be accessed by taxi or bus. . Oarai Isosaki-jinja is dedicated to two deities, Onamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto, who are believed to have created Japan. The shrine also has an impressive worship hall, guarded by statues of frogs instead of the typical lion-dogs. The frog is considered a symbol of good luck in Japan as it sounds like "safe return" or "return of something lost" in Japanese. The ocean is a [[Kannabi]] for the deities == See also == * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]] * [[Beppyo Shrine]] == References == {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Tourist attractions in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Ibaraki Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[sn:arai Isosaki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Kamiiso torii at Oarai Isosaki Jinja.jpg | caption = Kamiiso torii gate (神磯) of Ōarai Isosaki Shrine. | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]]
[[Sukunabikona]] }} '''Oarai Isosaki Shrine''' (大洗磯前神社, Ōarai Isosaki jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ōarai, Ibaraki|Ōarai City]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |date=2017-05-01 |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine - Japan National Tourism Organization |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |url-status=bot: unknown |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170501034955/https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |archive-date=2017-05-01 |access-date=2023-04-07 }} It has three [[torii]] gates leading into the ocean. It worships [[Sukunabikona]] a god of alcohol and medicine.{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Oarai+Isosaki+Shrine&pg=PA73 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}{{Cite web |title=A Study of Shinto: The Religion of the Japanese Nation |url=https://www.routledge.com/A-Study-of-Shinto-The-Religion-of-the-Japanese-Nation/Katu/p/book/9780415845762 |access-date=2023-11-18 |website=Routledge & CRC Press |language=en}}{{Rp|page=29}} and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]].{{Rp|page=29}} Both identified as [[Bhaisajyaguru]] in historical Buddhist traditions of the shrine.{{Rp|page=166}} It was historically a center of "Sea [[Shugendō|Shugendo]]" with many traditions here parallel to the [[Mountain worship]] linked traditional Shugendo..{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}{{rp|102}} The ocean is the Shintai of the shrine, it is a [[kannabi]]. [[File: Oarai isozaki shrine,Oarai isozaki jinjya haiden,oarai-town,japan.jpg|thumb|right|250px| Hall of worship of the Ōarai Isosaki Shrine.]]The shrine was founded in 856 It is also a [[Myojin Taisha]]. It has a nearby related shrine [[Sakatsura Isosaki Shrine]] dedicated to the same deities == Overview == The shrine is believed to have been established in 856 A.D. and was destroyed during the 16th-century war. It was later rebuilt in the 17th century. The shrine has three torii gates, each offering a unique view and environment. The Kamiiso-no-Torii is the most impressive gate, providing a prime location to watch the sunrise as it faces east. As the sun appears on the horizon, its rays shine through the gate, giving it a beautiful red hue. At night, the gate is equally stunning when illuminated by moonlight.{{Cite web |last=Organization |first=Japan National Tourism |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine {{!}} Travel Japan (Japan National Tourism Organization) |url=https://www.japan.travel/en/spot/1459/ |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=Travel Japan |language=en-us}} == Geographic location == Ōarai Isosaki Shrine is located on the [[Kantō region|Kantō]] Plain, northeast of Ibaraki Prefecture, situated next to the [[Pacific Ocean]]. Address: 〒311-1301, 6890 Isohama-chō, Ōarai-machi, Higashiibaraki-gun, Ibaraki-ken, Japan. Plans and satellite views of the sanctuary. {{Coord|36|18|57.07|N|140|35|14.81|E}} Plans and satellite views of the Kamiiso torii gate (神磯). {{Coord|36|18|53.8|N|140|35|22.2|E}} == History == The Ōarai Isosaki shrine has a long history and was established in the year 856. During the war from 1558 to 1570, the shrine was destroyed. However, it was rebuilt in 1690 by patrons, including [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]]. The [[Ibaraki Prefecture]] has designated the shrine as a cultural property. == Characteristics == The Oarai Isosaki-jinja shrine has three torii gates in separate locations. The main torii gate is a huge reinforced concrete structure that is 15.60 m high and 22.42 m wide, located on a path. Behind it, a second Torii rises, and as you pass this second torii, you see the shrine buildings. The third torii, Kamiiso Torii (神磯鳥居), is located on the coast and faces east towards the Pacific Ocean, it faces east to the ocean and the rising sun, representing the spot where the gods landed. The shrine is about two and a half kilometers away from Oarai Station and can be accessed by taxi or bus. . Oarai Isosaki-jinja is dedicated to two deities, Onamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto, who are believed to have created Japan. The shrine also has an impressive worship hall, guarded by statues of frogs instead of the typical lion-dogs. The frog is considered a symbol of good luck in Japan as it sounds like "safe return" or "return of something lost" in Japanese. The ocean is a [[Kannabi]] for the deities == See also == * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]] * [[Beppyo Shrine]] == References == {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Tourist attractions in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Ibaraki Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[sn:arai Isosaki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Oarai Isosaki Jinja}} [[es:Santuario Ōarai Isosaki]] [[ja:大洗磯前神社]] [[simple:Ōarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[zh:大洗矶前神社]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Kamiiso torii at Oarai Isosaki Jinja.jpg | caption = Kamiiso torii gate (神磯) of Ōarai Isosaki Shrine. | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]]
[[Sukunabikona]] }} '''Oarai Isosaki Shrine''' (大洗磯前神社, Ōarai Isosaki jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ōarai, Ibaraki|Ōarai City]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |date=2017-05-01 |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine - Japan National Tourism Organization |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |url-status=bot: unknown |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170501034955/https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |archive-date=2017-05-01 |access-date=2023-04-07 }} It has three [[torii]] gates leading into the ocean. It worships [[Sukunabikona]] a god of alcohol and medicine.{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Oarai+Isosaki+Shrine&pg=PA73 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}{{Cite web |title=A Study of Shinto: The Religion of the Japanese Nation |url=https://www.routledge.com/A-Study-of-Shinto-The-Religion-of-the-Japanese-Nation/Katu/p/book/9780415845762 |access-date=2023-11-18 |website=Routledge & CRC Press |language=en}}{{Rp|page=29}} and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]].{{Rp|page=29}} Both identified as [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Bhaisajyaguru]] in historical Buddhist traditions of the shrine.{{Rp|page=166}} It was historically a center of "Sea [[Shugendō|Shugendo]]" with many traditions here parallel to the [[Mountain worship]] linked traditional Shugendo..{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}{{rp|102}} The ocean is the Shintai of the shrine, it is a [[kannabi]]. [[File: Oarai isozaki shrine,Oarai isozaki jinjya haiden,oarai-town,japan.jpg|thumb|right|250px| Hall of worship of the Ōarai Isosaki Shrine.]]The shrine was founded in 856 It is also a [[Myojin Taisha]]. It has a nearby related shrine [[Sakatsura Isosaki Shrine]] dedicated to the same deities == Overview == The shrine is believed to have been established in 856 A.D. and was destroyed during the 16th-century war. It was later rebuilt in the 17th century. The shrine has three torii gates, each offering a unique view and environment. The Kamiiso-no-Torii is the most impressive gate, providing a prime location to watch the sunrise as it faces east. As the sun appears on the horizon, its rays shine through the gate, giving it a beautiful red hue. At night, the gate is equally stunning when illuminated by moonlight.{{Cite web |last=Organization |first=Japan National Tourism |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine {{!}} Travel Japan (Japan National Tourism Organization) |url=https://www.japan.travel/en/spot/1459/ |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=Travel Japan |language=en-us}} == Geographic location == Ōarai Isosaki Shrine is located on the [[Kantō region|Kantō]] Plain, northeast of Ibaraki Prefecture, situated next to the [[Pacific Ocean]]. Address: 〒311-1301, 6890 Isohama-chō, Ōarai-machi, Higashiibaraki-gun, Ibaraki-ken, Japan. Plans and satellite views of the sanctuary. {{Coord|36|18|57.07|N|140|35|14.81|E}} Plans and satellite views of the Kamiiso torii gate (神磯). {{Coord|36|18|53.8|N|140|35|22.2|E}} == History == The Ōarai Isosaki shrine has a long history and was established in the year 856. During the war from 1558 to 1570, the shrine was destroyed. However, it was rebuilt in 1690 by patrons, including [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]]. The [[Ibaraki Prefecture]] has designated the shrine as a cultural property. == Characteristics == The Oarai Isosaki-jinja shrine has three torii gates in separate locations. The main torii gate is a huge reinforced concrete structure that is 15.60 m high and 22.42 m wide, located on a path. Behind it, a second Torii rises, and as you pass this second torii, you see the shrine buildings. The third torii, Kamiiso Torii (神磯鳥居), is located on the coast and faces east towards the Pacific Ocean, it faces east to the ocean and the rising sun, representing the spot where the gods landed. The shrine is about two and a half kilometers away from Oarai Station and can be accessed by taxi or bus. . Oarai Isosaki-jinja is dedicated to two deities, Onamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto, who are believed to have created Japan. The shrine also has an impressive worship hall, guarded by statues of frogs instead of the typical lion-dogs. The frog is considered a symbol of good luck in Japan as it sounds like "safe return" or "return of something lost" in Japanese. The ocean is a [[Kannabi]] for the deities == See also == * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]] * [[Beppyo Shrine]] == References == {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Tourist attractions in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Ibaraki Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] }} [[sn:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[sn:arai Isosaki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Oarai Isosaki Jinja}} [[es:Santuario Ōarai Isosaki]] [[ja:大洗磯前神社]] [[simple:Ōarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[zh:大洗矶前神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Kamiiso torii at Oarai Isosaki Jinja.jpg | caption = Kamiiso torii gate (神磯) of Ōarai Isosaki Shrine. | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]]
[[Sukunabikona]] }} '''Oarai Isosaki Shrine''' (大洗磯前神社, Ōarai Isosaki jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ōarai, Ibaraki|Ōarai City]], [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |date=2017-05-01 |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine - Japan National Tourism Organization |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |url-status=bot: unknown |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170501034955/https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |archive-date=2017-05-01 |access-date=2023-04-07 }} It has three [[torii]] gates leading into the ocean. It worships [[Sukunabikona]] a god of alcohol and medicine.{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Oarai+Isosaki+Shrine&pg=PA73 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}{{Cite web |title=A Study of Shinto: The Religion of the Japanese Nation |url=https://www.routledge.com/A-Study-of-Shinto-The-Religion-of-the-Japanese-Nation/Katu/p/book/9780415845762 |access-date=2023-11-18 |website=Routledge & CRC Press |language=en}}{{Rp|page=29}} and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]].{{Rp|page=29}} Both identified as [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Bhaisajyaguru]] in historical Buddhist traditions of the shrine.{{Rp|page=166}} It was historically a center of "Sea [[Shugendō|Shugendo]]" with many traditions here parallel to the [[:en:Mountain worship|Mountain worship]] linked traditional Shugendo..{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}{{rp|102}} The ocean is the Shintai of the shrine, it is a [[kannabi]]. [[File: Oarai isozaki shrine,Oarai isozaki jinjya haiden,oarai-town,japan.jpg|thumb|right|250px| Hall of worship of the Ōarai Isosaki Shrine.]]The shrine was founded in 856 It is also a [[Myojin Taisha]]. It has a nearby related shrine [[Sakatsura Isosaki Shrine]] dedicated to the same deities == Overview == The shrine is believed to have been established in 856 A.D. and was destroyed during the 16th-century war. It was later rebuilt in the 17th century. The shrine has three torii gates, each offering a unique view and environment. The Kamiiso-no-Torii is the most impressive gate, providing a prime location to watch the sunrise as it faces east. As the sun appears on the horizon, its rays shine through the gate, giving it a beautiful red hue. At night, the gate is equally stunning when illuminated by moonlight.{{Cite web |last=Organization |first=Japan National Tourism |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine {{!}} Travel Japan (Japan National Tourism Organization) |url=https://www.japan.travel/en/spot/1459/ |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=Travel Japan |language=en-us}} == Geographic location == Ōarai Isosaki Shrine is located on the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō]] Plain, northeast of Ibaraki Prefecture, situated next to the [[Pacific Ocean]]. Address: 〒311-1301, 6890 Isohama-chō, Ōarai-machi, Higashiibaraki-gun, Ibaraki-ken, Japan. Plans and satellite views of the sanctuary. {{Coord|36|18|57.07|N|140|35|14.81|E}} Plans and satellite views of the Kamiiso torii gate (神磯). {{Coord|36|18|53.8|N|140|35|22.2|E}} == History == The Ōarai Isosaki shrine has a long history and was established in the year 856. During the war from 1558 to 1570, the shrine was destroyed. However, it was rebuilt in 1690 by patrons, including [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]]. The [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]] has designated the shrine as a cultural property. == Characteristics == The Oarai Isosaki-jinja shrine has three torii gates in separate locations. The main torii gate is a huge reinforced concrete structure that is 15.60 m high and 22.42 m wide, located on a path. Behind it, a second Torii rises, and as you pass this second torii, you see the shrine buildings. The third torii, Kamiiso Torii (神磯鳥居), is located on the coast and faces east towards the Pacific Ocean, it faces east to the ocean and the rising sun, representing the spot where the gods landed. The shrine is about two and a half kilometers away from Oarai Station and can be accessed by taxi or bus. . Oarai Isosaki-jinja is dedicated to two deities, Onamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto, who are believed to have created Japan. The shrine also has an impressive worship hall, guarded by statues of frogs instead of the typical lion-dogs. The frog is considered a symbol of good luck in Japan as it sounds like "safe return" or "return of something lost" in Japanese. The ocean is a [[Kannabi]] for the deities == See also == * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]] * [[Beppyo Shrine]] == References == {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Tourist attractions in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Ibaraki Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] }} [[sn:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[sn:arai Isosaki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Oarai Isosaki Jinja}} [[es:Santuario Ōarai Isosaki]] [[ja:大洗磯前神社]] [[simple:Ōarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[zh:大洗矶前神社]]

It worships [[Sukunabikona]] a god of alcohol and medicine.{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Oarai+Isosaki+Shrine&pg=PA73 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}{{Cite web |title=A Study of Shinto: The Religion of the Japanese Nation |url=https://www.routledge.com/A-Study-of-Shinto-The-Religion-of-the-Japanese-Nation/Katu/p/book/9780415845762 |access-date=2023-11-18 |website=Routledge & CRC Press |language=en}}{{Rp|page=29}} and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]].{{Rp|page=29}} Both identified as [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Bhaisajyaguru]] in historical Buddhist traditions of the shrine.{{Rp|page=166}} It was historically a center of "Sea [[Shugendō|Shugendo]]" with many traditions here parallel to the [[:en:Mountain worship|Mountain worship]] linked traditional Shugendo..{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}{{rp|102}} The ocean is the Shintai of the shrine, it is a [[kannabi]]. [[File: Oarai isozaki shrine,Oarai isozaki jinjya haiden,oarai-town,japan.jpg|thumb|right|250px| Hall of worship of the Ōarai Isosaki Shrine.]]The shrine was founded in 856 It is also a [[:en:Myojin Taisha|Myojin Taisha]]. It has a nearby related shrine [[Sakatsura Isosaki Shrine]] dedicated to the same deities == Overview == The shrine is believed to have been established in 856 A.D. and was destroyed during the 16th-century war. It was later rebuilt in the 17th century. The shrine has three torii gates, each offering a unique view and environment. The Kamiiso-no-Torii is the most impressive gate, providing a prime location to watch the sunrise as it faces east. As the sun appears on the horizon, its rays shine through the gate, giving it a beautiful red hue. At night, the gate is equally stunning when illuminated by moonlight.{{Cite web |last=Organization |first=Japan National Tourism |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine {{!}} Travel Japan (Japan National Tourism Organization) |url=https://www.japan.travel/en/spot/1459/ |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=Travel Japan |language=en-us}} == Geographic location == Ōarai Isosaki Shrine is located on the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō]] Plain, northeast of Ibaraki Prefecture, situated next to the [[:en:Pacific Ocean|Pacific Ocean]]. Address: 〒311-1301, 6890 Isohama-chō, Ōarai-machi, Higashiibaraki-gun, Ibaraki-ken, Japan. Plans and satellite views of the sanctuary. {{Coord|36|18|57.07|N|140|35|14.81|E}} Plans and satellite views of the Kamiiso torii gate (神磯). {{Coord|36|18|53.8|N|140|35|22.2|E}} == History == The Ōarai Isosaki shrine has a long history and was established in the year 856. During the war from 1558 to 1570, the shrine was destroyed. However, it was rebuilt in 1690 by patrons, including [[:en:Tokugawa Mitsukuni|Tokugawa Mitsukuni]]. The [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]] has designated the shrine as a cultural property. == Characteristics == The Oarai Isosaki-jinja shrine has three torii gates in separate locations. The main torii gate is a huge reinforced concrete structure that is 15.60 m high and 22.42 m wide, located on a path. Behind it, a second Torii rises, and as you pass this second torii, you see the shrine buildings. The third torii, Kamiiso Torii (神磯鳥居), is located on the coast and faces east towards the Pacific Ocean, it faces east to the ocean and the rising sun, representing the spot where the gods landed. The shrine is about two and a half kilometers away from Oarai Station and can be accessed by taxi or bus. . Oarai Isosaki-jinja is dedicated to two deities, Onamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto, who are believed to have created Japan. The shrine also has an impressive worship hall, guarded by statues of frogs instead of the typical lion-dogs. The frog is considered a symbol of good luck in Japan as it sounds like "safe return" or "return of something lost" in Japanese. The ocean is a [[Kannabi]] for the deities == See also == * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]] * [[Beppyo Shrine]] == References == {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Tourist attractions in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Ibaraki Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] }} [[sn:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[sn:arai Isosaki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Oarai Isosaki Jinja}} [[es:Santuario Ōarai Isosaki]] [[ja:大洗磯前神社]] [[simple:Ōarai Isosaki Shrine]] [[zh:大洗矶前神社]]

{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:大洗磯前神社 境内.JPG | caption = Kamiiso torii gate (神磯) of Ōarai Isosaki Shrine. | deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|qid=Q276944}} | prefecture= {{ill|Oarai|qid=Q1349185}} | coordinates= {{Coord|36.315853|N|140.587447|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Oarai Isosaki Shrine''' (大洗磯前神社, Ōarai Isosaki jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ōarai, Ibaraki|Ōarai City]], [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]], Japan.<ref name="mw-2017">{{Cite web |date=2017-05-01 |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine - Japan National Tourism Organization |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |url-status=bot: unknown |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170501034955/https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/oaraiisosakijinjashrine.html |archive-date=2017-05-01 |access-date=2023-04-07 }}</ref> It has three [[torii]] gates leading into the ocean.<ref name="mw-2017" />
It worships [[Sukunabikona]] a god of alcohol and medicine.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Oarai+Isosaki+Shrine&pg=PA73 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="routledge-crc-press-2023">{{Cite web |title=A Study of Shinto: The Religion of the Japanese Nation |url=https://www.routledge.com/A-Study-of-Shinto-The-Religion-of-the-Japanese-Nation/Katu/p/book/9780415845762 |access-date=2023-11-18 |website=Routledge & CRC Press |language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|page=29}} and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]].<ref name="routledge-crc-press-2023" />{{Rp|page=29}} Both identified as [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Bhaisajyaguru]] in historical Buddhist traditions of the shrine.<ref name="routledge-crc-press-2023" />{{Rp|page=166}}
It was historically a center of "Sea [[Shugendō|Shugendo]]" with many traditions here parallel to the [[:en:Mountain worship|Mountain worship]] linked traditional Shugendo..<ref>{{Cite book |last=Rambelli |first=F |title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing |year=2018 |isbn=978-1350062870 |location=Camden |pages=}}</ref>{{rp|102}}
The ocean is the Shintai of the shrine, it is a [[kannabi]].
[[File: Oarai isozaki shrine,Oarai isozaki jinjya haiden,oarai-town,japan.jpg|thumb|right|250px| Hall of worship of the Ōarai Isosaki Shrine.]]The shrine was founded in 856<ref name="mw-2017" />
It is also a [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]. It has a nearby related shrine [[Sakatsura Isosaki Shrine]] dedicated to the same deities
== Overview == The shrine is believed to have been established in 856 A.D. and was destroyed during the 16th-century war. It was later rebuilt in the 17th century. The shrine has three torii gates, each offering a unique view and environment.<ref name="mw-2017" />
The Kamiiso-no-Torii is the most impressive gate, providing a prime location to watch the sunrise as it faces east. As the sun appears on the horizon, its rays shine through the gate, giving it a beautiful red hue. At night, the gate is equally stunning when illuminated by moonlight.<ref name="organization-2023">{{Cite web |last=Organization |first=Japan National Tourism |title=Oarai Isosaki-jinja Shrine {{!}} Travel Japan (Japan National Tourism Organization) |url=https://www.japan.travel/en/spot/1459/ |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=Travel Japan |language=en-us}}</ref>
== Geographic location == Ōarai Isosaki Shrine is located on the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō]] Plain, northeast of Ibaraki Prefecture, situated next to the [[:en:Pacific Ocean|Pacific Ocean]].
Address: 〒311-1301, 6890 Isohama-chō, Ōarai-machi, Higashiibaraki-gun, Ibaraki-ken, Japan.
Plans and satellite views of the sanctuary. {{Coord|36|18|57.07|N|140|35|14.81|E}}
Plans and satellite views of the Kamiiso torii gate (神磯). {{Coord|36|18|53.8|N|140|35|22.2|E}}
== History == The Ōarai Isosaki shrine has a long history and was established in the year 856.<ref name="mw-2017" />
During the war from 1558 to 1570, the shrine was destroyed. However, it was rebuilt in 1690 by patrons,<ref name="mw-2017" /> including [[:en:Tokugawa Mitsukuni|Tokugawa Mitsukuni]].<ref name="mw-2017" />
The [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]] has designated the shrine as a cultural property.<ref name="mw-2017" />
== Characteristics == The Oarai Isosaki-jinja shrine has three torii gates in separate locations. <ref name="mw-2017" />
The main torii gate is a huge reinforced concrete structure that is 15.60 m high and 22.42 m wide, located on a path. <ref name="mw-2017" />
Behind it, a second Torii rises, and as you pass this second torii, you see the shrine buildings. <ref name="mw-2017" />
The third torii, Kamiiso Torii (神磯鳥居), is located on the coast and faces east towards the Pacific Ocean, it faces east to the ocean and the rising sun, representing the spot where the gods landed. The shrine is about two and a half kilometers away from Oarai Station and can be accessed by taxi or bus. .<ref name="organization-2023" />
Oarai Isosaki-jinja is dedicated to two deities, Onamuchi-no-Mikoto and Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto, who are believed to have created Japan.<ref name="organization-2023" /> The shrine also has an impressive worship hall, guarded by statues of frogs instead of the typical lion-dogs.<ref name="mw-2017" /> The frog is considered a symbol of good luck in Japan as it sounds like "safe return" or "return of something lost" in Japanese.<ref name="organization-2023" />
The ocean is a [[Kannabi]] for the deities
== See also == * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]] * [[Beppyo Shrine]]
